Assignment 1 - Deep Neural Networks¶

Dataset: Uber & Lyft Rideshare Dataset – Boston, MA¶

Task: Regression - Predicting Ride Price¶

4.1 Dataset Selection¶

Dataset Description¶

Name: Uber and Lyft Dataset Boston, MA
Source: Kaggle – https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/brllrb/uber-and-lyft-dataset-boston-ma
Collection Period: November 26, 2018 to December 18, 2018 (22 days)

This dataset was collected via real-time API queries to Uber and Lyft services at several hotspot locations in Boston, MA, sampled every 5 minutes over 22 days. It captures ride-level pricing data along with concurrent weather conditions.

Raw dataset: ~693,000 rows × 57 columns
After cleaning: 637,628 rows used for training - full dataset, no sampling.

Problem Type¶

Regression — We predict the continuous price variable (ride fare in USD).

Feature Type Description
distance Numeric Trip distance in miles
surge_multiplier Numeric Demand-based pricing multiplier
cab_type_enc Numeric 0=Lyft, 1=Uber
name_enc Numeric Ride tier encoded (0–11)
source_enc Numeric Pickup neighbourhood encoded (0–11)
destination_enc Numeric Dropoff neighbourhood encoded (0–11)
hour Numeric Hour of day (0–23)
day_of_week Numeric 1=Monday to 7=Sunday
apparentTemperature Numeric Feels-like temperature in °F
humidity Numeric Humidity (0–1)
windGust Numeric Peak wind gust in mph
visibility Numeric Visibility in miles
precipProbability Numeric Probability of precipitation (0–1)
cloudCover Numeric Cloud cover (0–1)

Total: 14 features

Primary Evaluation Metric: RMSE¶

Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is chosen as the primary metric because:

  • Fare prediction errors have a direct monetary impact - RMSE penalises large errors more than MAE, which is important when a $10 prediction error is much worse than a $1 error.
  • It is in the same unit as the target (USD), making it intuitively interpretable.
  • It is the standard metric used in Kaggle's official NYC Taxi Fare Prediction competition, making it an industry norm for ride pricing tasks.

Imports¶

In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import time
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, LabelEncoder

np.random.seed(42)
print("All imports successful.")

All imports successful.
In [2]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 1: Load the dataset
#
# Download from Kaggle:
#   https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/brllrb/uber-and-lyft-dataset-boston-ma
# Place rideshare_kaggle.csv in the same directory as this notebook.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

df_raw = pd.read_csv('data/rideshare_kaggle.csv')
print(f"Raw dataset shape: {df_raw.shape}")
print(f"\nColumns:\n{list(df_raw.columns)}")

4.2 Data Preprocessing¶

In [5]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Step 2.1: Full Dataset Overview — All Columns
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

print("=" * 60)
print("SHAPE:", df_raw.shape)
print("=" * 60)

print("\n--- COLUMN TYPES ---")
print(df_raw.dtypes)

print("\n--- MISSING VALUES (only columns with missing) ---")
missing = df_raw.isnull().sum()
missing = missing[missing > 0]
print(missing)
print(f"\nTotal columns with missing values: {len(missing)}")

print("\n--- CATEGORICAL COLUMNS: Unique Values ---")
df_raw.drop(columns=['id', 'datetime'], inplace=True)
cat_cols = df_raw.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns.tolist()
for col in cat_cols:
    print(f"  {col}: {df_raw[col].nunique()} unique → {df_raw[col].unique().tolist()}")

print("\n--- NUMERIC COLUMNS: Stats ---")
pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.4f}'.format)
print(df_raw.describe().T)   # .T transposes — easier to read one column per row

visibility.1                         9.9960         10.0000

temperatureMin                      38.8800         43.1000

In [6]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Step 2.2: EDA — Correlation Analysis (on raw numeric cols)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Numeric columns from df_raw that we are considering as features
candidate_numeric_cols = [
    'distance', 'surge_multiplier', 'hour', 'day',
    'apparentTemperature', 'humidity', 'windSpeed', 'windGust',
    'visibility', 'precipIntensity', 'precipProbability', 'cloudCover',
    'dewPoint', 'pressure', 'windBearing', 'uvIndex', 'ozone',
    'price'   # target 
]

corr_matrix = df_raw[candidate_numeric_cols].corr()

# Plot heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm',
            center=0, linewidths=0.5)
plt.title('Feature Correlation Matrix (candidate numeric columns)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# windSpeed vs windGust specifically
print(f"Correlation — windSpeed vs windGust: "
      f"{df_raw['windSpeed'].corr(df_raw['windGust']):.4f}")

# All correlations with price, sorted
print(f"\nCorrelation with price (target):")
print(corr_matrix['price'].sort_values(ascending=False))

Correlation — windSpeed vs windGust: 0.9376

Correlation with price (target):
price                  1.0000
distance               0.3451
surge_multiplier       0.2405
visibility             0.0015
windGust               0.0012
windSpeed              0.0010
cloudCover             0.0009
pressure               0.0009
hour                   0.0006
ozone                  0.0005
precipIntensity        0.0002
apparentTemperature   -0.0002
precipProbability     -0.0002
uvIndex               -0.0004
dewPoint              -0.0006
day                   -0.0007
humidity              -0.0012
windBearing           -0.0014
Name: price, dtype: float64
In [7]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 2.3: Final Column Selection
#
# Decisions from stats + correlation matrix:
#
# DROPPED (multicollinearity):
#   windSpeed       — 0.94 corr with windGust
#   dewPoint        — 0.85 corr with apparentTemperature, 0.75 with humidity
#   precipIntensity — 0.84 corr with precipProbability; precipProbability
#                     is more interpretable and has better range
#
# DROPPED (no causal link to price):
#   pressure, windBearing, ozone — no logical pathway to ride pricing
#   uvIndex — 75th pct = 0, max = 2; no signal in Boston December
#
# KEPT (weather → demand → surge → price logic):
#   apparentTemperature, humidity, windGust, visibility,
#   precipProbability, cloudCover
#   Rationale: weather affects rider discomfort and booking decisions,
#   which drives surge multiplier, which directly affects price.
#   The MLP can learn these non-linear relationships even where
#   linear correlation with price appears near-zero.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

COLS = [
    # Ride features
    'cab_type',             # Uber vs Lyft
    'name',                 # ride tier: UberX, Black, Shared, Lyft XL...
    'source',               # pickup neighbourhood (12 unique Boston areas)
    'destination',          # dropoff neighbourhood (12 unique Boston areas)
    'distance',             # trip distance in miles — strongest predictor (r=0.35)
    'surge_multiplier',     # demand multiplier — direct pricing component (r=0.24)

    # Time features
    'hour',                 # 0–23 — peak demand patterns
    'day',                  # 1–30 — end-of-month / holiday patterns

    # Weather features — ride-level conditions
    # pathway: weather → discomfort → demand → surge → price
    'apparentTemperature',  # feels-like °F — rider comfort signal
    'humidity',             # 0–1 — discomfort driver
    'windGust',             # peak wind mph — dropped windSpeed (r=0.94 with windGust)
    'visibility',           # miles — captures fog independently of rain
    'precipProbability',    # 0–1 — best precipitation signal (dropped precipIntensity,
                            #        r=0.84 with precipProbability)
    'cloudCover',           # 0–1 — general weather gloominess

    # TARGET
    'price',
]

df = df_raw[COLS].copy()
print(f"Selected columns shape: {df.shape}")
print(f"Number of features (excluding target): {len(COLS) - 1}")
print(f"\nMissing values:\n{df.isnull().sum()}")

Selected columns shape: (693071, 15)
Number of features (excluding target): 14

Missing values:
cab_type                   0
name                       0
source                     0
destination                0
distance                   0
surge_multiplier           0
hour                       0
day                        0
apparentTemperature        0
humidity                   0
windGust                   0
visibility                 0
precipProbability          0
cloudCover                 0
price                  55095
dtype: int64
In [8]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 3: Handle Missing Values
#
# Only 'price' has missing values (55,095 rows = ~8% of data)
# These rows have no target value — cannot be used for training.
# Imputing a target variable is not valid — drop them.
# All 14 features are fully complete — no feature imputation needed.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

df.dropna(subset=['price'], inplace=True)

print(f"Shape after dropping missing price rows: {df.shape}")
print(f"Rows removed: {len(df_raw) - len(df)}")
print(f"\nMissing values after drop:\n{df.isnull().sum()}")
print(f"\nPrice range: ${df['price'].min():.2f} – ${df['price'].max():.2f}")

Shape after dropping missing price rows: (637976, 15)
Rows removed: 55095

name                   0
source                 0
destination            0

hour                   0
day                    0
apparentTemperature    0
humidity               0
windGust               0
visibility             0
precipProbability      0
cloudCover             0
price                  0

In [10]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 4.1: Outlier Check
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(16, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

numeric_features = [
    'price', 'distance', 'surge_multiplier',
    'apparentTemperature', 'humidity', 'windGust',
    'visibility', 'precipProbability'
]

for ax, col in zip(axes, numeric_features):
    ax.boxplot(df[col].dropna(), vert=True)
    ax.set_title(col)
    ax.set_ylabel('Value')

plt.suptitle('Boxplots — Outlier Check', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Print IQR-based outlier counts for each numeric column
print("Outlier counts (beyond 1.5×IQR):")
for col in numeric_features:
    Q1  = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3  = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    n_outliers = ((df[col] < lower) | (df[col] > upper)).sum()
    print(f"  {col:<22}: {n_outliers:>6} outliers  "
          f"| valid range [{lower:.2f}, {upper:.2f}]")

Outlier counts (beyond 1.5×IQR):
  price                 :   5589 outliers  | valid range [-11.25, 42.75]
  distance              :   6672 outliers  | valid range [-1.22, 5.42]
  surge_multiplier      :  20975 outliers  | valid range [1.00, 1.00]
  apparentTemperature   :  49208 outliers  | valid range [19.76, 52.16]
  humidity              :      0 outliers  | valid range [0.28, 1.24]
  windGust              :  15501 outliers  | valid range [-7.46, 23.26]
  visibility            : 125857 outliers  | valid range [6.09, 12.34]
  precipProbability     : 138748 outliers  | valid range [0.00, 0.00]
In [11]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 4.2: Distribution Check
# Boxplot flagged many outliers — but IQR method assumes
# roughly normal distribution. Let's verify before acting.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
from scipy import stats

numeric_features = [
    'price', 'distance', 'surge_multiplier',
    'apparentTemperature', 'humidity', 'windGust',
    'visibility', 'precipProbability'
]

# Plot histograms + KDE to visually check distribution shape
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(16, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for ax, col in zip(axes, numeric_features):
    ax.hist(df[col], bins=50, density=True,
            color='steelblue', edgecolor='white', alpha=0.7)
    kde_x = np.linspace(df[col].min(), df[col].max(), 200)
    kde   = stats.gaussian_kde(df[col])
    ax.plot(kde_x, kde(kde_x), color='darkorange', linewidth=2)
    ax.set_title(col)
    ax.set_xlabel('Value')
    ax.set_ylabel('Density')

plt.suptitle('Distribution Check — Histogram + KDE', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Skewness and Kurtosis — quantitative normality indicators
# Normal distribution: skewness ≈ 0, kurtosis ≈ 0
# |skewness| > 1 → significantly skewed → IQR method unreliable
# kurtosis  > 3 → heavy tails → more extreme values than normal
print(f"\n{'Column':<25} {'Skewness':>10} {'Kurtosis':>10} {'Verdict':>20}")
print("-" * 70)
for col in numeric_features:
    skew = df[col].skew()
    kurt = df[col].kurt()
    if abs(skew) < 0.5:
        verdict = 'approx normal'
    elif abs(skew) < 1.0:
        verdict = 'moderately skewed'
    else:
        verdict = 'highly skewed'
    print(f"  {col:<23} {skew:>10.4f} {kurt:>10.4f} {verdict:>20}")

Column                      Skewness   Kurtosis              Verdict
----------------------------------------------------------------------
  price                       1.0457     1.2248        highly skewed
  distance                    0.8168     1.1515    moderately skewed
  surge_multiplier            7.9690    73.7982        highly skewed
  apparentTemperature        -0.3936     1.1377        approx normal
  humidity                   -0.0748    -1.0271        approx normal
  windGust                    1.1184     1.1532        highly skewed
  visibility                 -1.5081     0.6402        highly skewed
  precipProbability           2.0293     2.3416        highly skewed
In [12]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 4.3: Outlier Removal — Domain-Driven Approach
#
# Confirmed from Step 4.2: 6/8 features are non-normal
# (skewness > 1 or < -1). IQR method is unreliable for
# skewed and zero-inflated distributions.
#
# Strategy: use domain knowledge + hard physical bounds
# instead of statistical thresholds.
#
# price           : min $2.50, max $97.50 — all valid ride
#                   tiers. No removal.
# distance        : min 0.02 miles is suspicious — rides
#                   under 0.1 miles are likely cancelled or
#                   GPS errors. Remove these only.
# surge_multiplier: 1.0–3.0 — Uber/Lyft documented range.
#                   All valid. No removal.
# apparentTemperature: 12°F–57°F — real Boston Nov-Dec
#                   weather. No removal.
# humidity        : 0.38–0.96 — physically valid. No removal.
# windGust        : 0.8–27.25 mph — real winter gusts.
#                   No removal.
# visibility      : 0.717–10.0 miles — API caps at 10.
#                   All valid. No removal.
# precipProbability: 0.0–1.0 — bounded by definition.
#                   No removal.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

before = len(df)

# Only domain-justified removal: rides under 0.1 miles
# are physically implausible — likely GPS errors or
# cancelled rides that were never actually taken
df = df[df['distance'] >= 0.1].copy()

after = len(df)

print(f"Rows removed (distance < 0.1 miles): {before - after}")
print(f"Shape after outlier handling: {df.shape}")
print(f"\nFinal ranges after cleaning:")
print(f"  price       : ${df['price'].min():.2f}  – ${df['price'].max():.2f}")
print(f"  distance    : {df['distance'].min():.2f} – {df['distance'].max():.2f} miles")
print(f"  surge_mult  : {df['surge_multiplier'].min():.2f} – {df['surge_multiplier'].max():.2f}")
print(f"  apparentTemp: {df['apparentTemperature'].min():.2f}°F – {df['apparentTemperature'].max():.2f}°F")
print(f"  windGust    : {df['windGust'].min():.2f} – {df['windGust'].max():.2f} mph")
print(f"  visibility  : {df['visibility'].min():.3f} – {df['visibility'].max():.2f} miles")

Rows removed (distance < 0.1 miles): 348
Shape after outlier handling: (637628, 15)

Final ranges after cleaning:
  price       : $2.50  – $97.50
  distance    : 0.12 – 7.86 miles
  surge_mult  : 1.00 – 3.00
  apparentTemp: 12.13°F – 57.22°F
  windGust    : 0.80 – 27.25 mph
  visibility  : 0.717 – 10.00 miles
In [13]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 5.0: Determine Peak Hours from Data
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

hourly_rides = df.groupby('hour').size().reset_index(name='ride_count')

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))
plt.bar(hourly_rides['hour'], hourly_rides['ride_count'],
        color='steelblue', edgecolor='white')
plt.title('Ride Volume by Hour of Day')
plt.xlabel('Hour (0–23)')
plt.ylabel('Number of Rides')
plt.xticks(range(0, 24))
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Ride count by hour:")
print(hourly_rides.to_string(index=False))

In [14]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 5.1 Finding: Ride volume is relatively flat across
# hours — no classic AM/PM peak pattern exists in this data.
# This is because:
#   1. Data was collected via API queries every 5 mins at
#      fixed locations — uniform sampling, not demand-driven
#   2. Holiday season (Nov-Dec) shifts patterns toward
#      late-night entertainment traffic
#
# Data-driven hour segmentation based on actual ride counts:
#   is_late_night : hours 22–23 and 0–4 (>25k rides, highest)
#   is_low_demand : hours 5–8 and 19–21 (lowest volume <26k)
#   (midday 9–18 serves as the baseline — no flag needed)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

df['is_late_night'] = df['hour'].apply(
    lambda h: 1 if (h >= 22) or (h <= 4) else 0
)

df['is_low_demand'] = df['hour'].apply(
    lambda h: 1 if (5 <= h <= 8) or (19 <= h <= 21) else 0
)

print("is_late_night distribution:")
print(df['is_late_night'].value_counts().sort_index())
print(f"\nis_low_demand distribution:")
print(df['is_low_demand'].value_counts().sort_index())

is_late_night distribution:
is_late_night
0    447105
1    190523
Name: count, dtype: int64

is_low_demand distribution:
is_low_demand
0    468750
1    168878
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [17]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 5.2: Encode Categorical Variables
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Derive day_of_week from df_raw timestamp
# Using df_raw.loc[df.index] to match only surviving rows
# after filtering steps
# 1=Monday, 2=Tuesday, 3=Wednesday, 4=Thursday,
# 5=Friday, 6=Saturday, 7=Sunday
df['day_of_week'] = pd.to_datetime(
    df_raw.loc[df.index, 'timestamp'], unit='s'
).dt.dayofweek + 1

df['is_weekend'] = (df['day_of_week'] >= 6).astype(int)

print("Day of week distribution (1=Mon, 7=Sun):")
print(df['day_of_week'].value_counts().sort_index())
print(f"\nWeekend rides : {df['is_weekend'].sum():,}")
print(f"Weekday rides : {(df['is_weekend'] == 0).sum():,}")

# --- Encode Categorical Columns ---
le_cab  = LabelEncoder()
le_name = LabelEncoder()
le_src  = LabelEncoder()
le_dst  = LabelEncoder()

df['cab_type_enc']    = le_cab.fit_transform(df['cab_type'])
df['name_enc']        = le_name.fit_transform(df['name'])
df['source_enc']      = le_src.fit_transform(df['source'])
df['destination_enc'] = le_dst.fit_transform(df['destination'])

# Print encoding mappings
print("\ncab_type encoding:")
for cls, enc in zip(le_cab.classes_, le_cab.transform(le_cab.classes_)):
    print(f"  {enc} → {cls}")

print("\nname encoding:")
for cls, enc in zip(le_name.classes_, le_name.transform(le_name.classes_)):
    print(f"  {enc} → {cls}")

print("\nsource encoding:")
for cls, enc in zip(le_src.classes_, le_src.transform(le_src.classes_)):
    print(f"  {enc} → {cls}")

print("\ndestination encoding:")
for cls, enc in zip(le_dst.classes_, le_dst.transform(le_dst.classes_)):
    print(f"  {enc} → {cls}")

In [18]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STEP 6: Define Final Feature Matrix + Train-Test Split
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FEATURES = [
    # Ride features
    'distance',           # strongest predictor (r=0.35)
    'surge_multiplier',   # direct pricing component (r=0.24)
    'cab_type_enc',       # Lyft=0, Uber=1
    'name_enc',           # ride tier 0–11
    'source_enc',         # pickup neighbourhood 0–11
    'destination_enc',    # dropoff neighbourhood 0–11

    # Time features
    'hour',               # 0–23
    'day_of_week',        # 1=Mon, 7=Sun

    # Weather features
    'apparentTemperature',  # feels-like °F
    'humidity',             # 0–1
    'windGust',             # peak wind mph
    'visibility',           # miles
    'precipProbability',    # 0–1
    'cloudCover',           # 0–1
]

TARGET = 'price'

X = df[FEATURES].values
y = df[TARGET].values.reshape(-1, 1)

print(f"Feature matrix shape : {X.shape}")
print(f"Target vector shape  : {y.shape}")
print(f"Number of features   : {len(FEATURES)}")
print(f"\nFeatures: {FEATURES}")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Train-Test Split — 80/20
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.20, random_state=42
)

print(f"\nTrain size : {X_train.shape[0]:,} rows")
print(f"Test size  : {X_test.shape[0]:,} rows")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Feature Scaling — StandardScaler
# Fit on train only, transform both train and test
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled  = scaler.transform(X_test)

print(f"\nX_train scaled — mean ≈ {X_train_scaled.mean():.4f}")
print(f"X_train scaled — std  ≈ {X_train_scaled.std():.4f}")

Feature matrix shape : (637628, 14)
Target vector shape  : (637628, 1)
Number of features   : 14

Features: ['distance', 'surge_multiplier', 'cab_type_enc', 'name_enc', 'source_enc', 'destination_enc', 'hour', 'day_of_week', 'apparentTemperature', 'humidity', 'windGust', 'visibility', 'precipProbability', 'cloudCover']

Train size : 510,102 rows
Test size  : 127,526 rows

X_train scaled — mean ≈ -0.0000
X_train scaled — std  ≈ 1.0000

4.3 Baseline Model: Linear Regression from Scratch¶

In [19]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 4.3 Baseline Model: Linear Regression from Scratch
#
# Implementation: Mini-batch Gradient Descent
# Model: y_hat = X @ w + b
# Loss:  MSE = (1/n) * sum((y_hat - y)^2)
# Update: w = w - lr * dw
#         b = b - lr * db
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class LinearRegressionScratch:
    """
    Linear Regression from scratch using mini-batch gradient descent.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    learning_rate : float
    n_epochs      : int   — number of full passes through training data
    batch_size    : int   — number of samples per mini-batch
    """

    def __init__(self, learning_rate=0.01, n_epochs=100, batch_size=512):
        self.lr          = learning_rate
        self.n_epochs    = n_epochs
        self.batch_size  = batch_size
        self.weights     = None
        self.bias        = None
        self.loss_history = []   # stores loss at end of each epoch

    def _initialize_weights(self, n_features):
        """Initialize weights to small random values, bias to 0."""
        self.weights = np.random.randn(n_features, 1) * 0.01
        self.bias    = 0.0

    def _forward(self, X):
        """Forward pass: compute predictions."""
        return X @ self.weights + self.bias   # (n, 1)

    def _compute_loss(self, y_hat, y):
        """
        Mean Squared Error — used as training loss.
        Note: RMSE (sqrt of MSE) is our primary evaluation metric
        and will be reported in Section 4.5. MSE is used here
        because it has simpler gradients than RMSE.
        """
        return np.mean((y_hat - y) ** 2)

    def _compute_gradients(self, X, y_hat, y):
        """
        Gradients of MSE w.r.t weights and bias.
        dL/dw = (2/n) * X.T @ (y_hat - y)
        dL/db = (2/n) * sum(y_hat - y)
        """
        n     = y.shape[0]
        error = y_hat - y                       # (n, 1)
        dw    = (2 / n) * (X.T @ error)        # (n_features, 1)
        db    = (2 / n) * np.sum(error)        # scalar
        return dw, db

    def fit(self, X, y):
        """
        Training loop:
        For each epoch:
            Shuffle data
            Split into mini-batches
            For each mini-batch:
                forward → loss → gradients → update weights
        Store epoch loss at end of each epoch.
        """
        n_samples  = X.shape[0]
        n_features = X.shape[1]
        self._initialize_weights(n_features)
        self.loss_history = []

        for epoch in range(self.n_epochs):
            # Shuffle training data at start of each epoch
            indices = np.random.permutation(n_samples)
            X_shuffled = X[indices]
            y_shuffled = y[indices]

            epoch_loss = 0.0
            n_batches  = 0

            # Mini-batch loop
            for start in range(0, n_samples, self.batch_size):
                end     = start + self.batch_size
                X_batch = X_shuffled[start:end]
                y_batch = y_shuffled[start:end]

                # Forward pass
                y_hat = self._forward(X_batch)

                # Compute batch loss
                batch_loss  = self._compute_loss(y_hat, y_batch)
                epoch_loss += batch_loss
                n_batches  += 1

                # Compute gradients
                dw, db = self._compute_gradients(X_batch, y_hat, y_batch)

                # Update weights: w = w - lr * grad
                self.weights -= self.lr * dw
                self.bias    -= self.lr * db

            # Store average loss across all batches for this epoch
            self.loss_history.append(epoch_loss / n_batches)

            if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
                print(f"  Epoch {epoch+1:>4}/{self.n_epochs} "
                      f"| MSE Loss: {self.loss_history[-1]:.4f}")

        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        """Return predictions for input X."""
        return self._forward(X)


print("LinearRegressionScratch class defined.")

LinearRegressionScratch class defined.
In [20]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Train Linear Regression
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

lin_reg = LinearRegressionScratch(
    learning_rate=0.01,
    n_epochs=100,
    batch_size=512
)
lin_reg.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

baseline_train_time = time.time() - start_baseline
print(f"\nTraining complete in {baseline_train_time:.2f}s")

4.4 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) from Scratch¶

In [23]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 4.4 Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) from Scratch
#
# Architecture : [14 → 64 → 32 → 1]
# Hidden layers: ReLU activation
# Output layer : Linear activation (no activation) — regression
# Loss         : MSE (RMSE reported in 4.5)
# Training     : Mini-batch gradient descent
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class MLP:
    """
    Multi-Layer Perceptron for Regression, implemented from scratch.

    Parameters
    ----------
    layer_dims    : list  e.g. [14, 64, 32, 1]
                    [input_dim, hidden1, hidden2, ..., output_dim]
    learning_rate : float
    n_epochs      : int   — number of full passes through training data
    batch_size    : int   — number of samples per mini-batch
    """

    def __init__(self, layer_dims, learning_rate=0.01,
                 n_epochs=100, batch_size=512):
        self.layer_dims   = layer_dims
        self.lr           = learning_rate
        self.n_epochs     = n_epochs
        self.batch_size   = batch_size
        self.params       = {}   # W1, b1, W2, b2, ...
        self.loss_history = []
        self.L            = len(layer_dims) - 1  # number of layers

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 1. Initialize Parameters
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def initialize_parameters(self):
        """
        Random initialisation — small random values for weights,
        bias initialised to 0.
        W[l] ~ N(0, 0.01)
        b[l] = 0
        """
        np.random.seed(42)
        for l in range(1, self.L + 1):
            n_in  = self.layer_dims[l - 1]
            n_out = self.layer_dims[l]
            self.params[f'W{l}'] = np.random.randn(n_in, n_out) * 0.01
            self.params[f'b{l}'] = np.zeros((1, n_out))

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 2. Activation Functions
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    @staticmethod
    def relu(Z):
        """ReLU: max(0, Z) — used for hidden layers."""
        return np.maximum(0, Z)

    @staticmethod
    def relu_derivative(Z):
        """Derivative of ReLU: 1 if Z > 0, else 0."""
        return (Z > 0).astype(float)

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 3. Forward Propagation
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def forward_propagation(self, X):
        """
        Compute activations through all layers.
        Hidden layers : ReLU
        Output layer  : Linear (no activation) — regression
        Returns: y_hat (predictions), cache (Z and A per layer)
        """
        cache = {}
        A     = X
        cache['A0'] = X

        for l in range(1, self.L + 1):
            W = self.params[f'W{l}']
            b = self.params[f'b{l}']
            Z = A @ W + b                    # pre-activation

            if l < self.L:
                A = self.relu(Z)             # hidden layers: ReLU
            else:
                A = Z                        # output layer: linear

            cache[f'Z{l}'] = Z
            cache[f'A{l}'] = A

        return A, cache

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 4. Loss
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    @staticmethod
    def compute_loss(y_hat, y):
        """
        Mean Squared Error — used as training loss.
        Note: RMSE reported in Section 4.5 for interpretability.
        MSE used here because gradients are simpler than RMSE.
        """
        return np.mean((y_hat - y) ** 2)

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 5. Backward Propagation
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def backward_propagation(self, y, cache):
        """
        Compute gradients using chain rule, layer by layer.
        Returns: grads dict with dW[l] and db[l] for each layer.

        Output layer (linear):
            dL/dZ_L = (2/n) * (y_hat - y)
        Hidden layers (ReLU):
            dZ[l] = dA[l] * ReLU'(Z[l])
        Parameter gradients:
            dW[l] = A[l-1].T @ dZ[l]
            db[l] = sum(dZ[l], axis=0)
        Propagate to previous layer:
            dA[l-1] = dZ[l] @ W[l].T
        """
        grads  = {}
        n      = y.shape[0]
        A_last = cache[f'A{self.L}']

        # Gradient of MSE w.r.t output — linear activation so dA = dZ
        dA = (2 / n) * (A_last - y)

        for l in reversed(range(1, self.L + 1)):
            Z_l    = cache[f'Z{l}']
            A_prev = cache[f'A{l-1}']
            W_l    = self.params[f'W{l}']

            if l == self.L:
                # Output layer: linear activation → dZ = dA
                dZ = dA
            else:
                # Hidden layer: apply ReLU derivative
                dZ = dA * self.relu_derivative(Z_l)

            # Parameter gradients
            grads[f'dW{l}'] = A_prev.T @ dZ
            grads[f'db{l}'] = np.sum(dZ, axis=0, keepdims=True)

            # Propagate gradient to previous layer
            dA = dZ @ W_l.T

        return grads

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 6. Fit (Training Loop)
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def fit(self, X, y):
        """
        Training loop with mini-batch gradient descent.
        For each epoch:
            Shuffle data
            Split into mini-batches
            For each mini-batch:
                forward → loss → backward → update weights
        Store average epoch loss in loss_history.
        """
        n_samples = X.shape[0]
        self.initialize_parameters()
        self.loss_history = []

        for epoch in range(self.n_epochs):
            # Shuffle data at start of each epoch
            indices    = np.random.permutation(n_samples)
            X_shuffled = X[indices]
            y_shuffled = y[indices]

            epoch_loss = 0.0
            n_batches  = 0

            for start in range(0, n_samples, self.batch_size):
                end     = start + self.batch_size
                X_batch = X_shuffled[start:end]
                y_batch = y_shuffled[start:end]

                # Forward pass
                y_hat, cache = self.forward_propagation(X_batch)

                # Compute batch loss
                batch_loss  = self.compute_loss(y_hat, y_batch)
                epoch_loss += batch_loss
                n_batches  += 1

                # Backward pass
                grads = self.backward_propagation(y_batch, cache)

                # Update weights: w = w - lr * grad
                for l in range(1, self.L + 1):
                    self.params[f'W{l}'] -= self.lr * grads[f'dW{l}']
                    self.params[f'b{l}'] -= self.lr * grads[f'db{l}']

            # Store average loss across all batches for this epoch
            self.loss_history.append(epoch_loss / n_batches)

            if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0:
                print(f"  Epoch {epoch+1:>4}/{self.n_epochs} "
                      f"| MSE Loss: {self.loss_history[-1]:.4f}")

        return self

    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # 7. Predict
    # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    def predict(self, X):
        """Return predictions for input X."""
        y_hat, _ = self.forward_propagation(X)
        return y_hat


print("MLP class defined.")

MLP class defined.
In [24]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Train MLP
# Architecture: [14 input → 64 → 32 → 1 output]
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

n_features   = X_train_scaled.shape[1]
ARCHITECTURE = [n_features, 64, 32, 1]

print(f"MLP Architecture : {ARCHITECTURE}")
print(f"  Layer 1 (hidden): {ARCHITECTURE[0]} → {ARCHITECTURE[1]}, ReLU")
print(f"  Layer 2 (hidden): {ARCHITECTURE[1]} → {ARCHITECTURE[2]}, ReLU")
print(f"  Layer 3 (output): {ARCHITECTURE[2]} → {ARCHITECTURE[3]}, Linear")
print()
print("Training MLP...")

start_mlp = time.time()

mlp = MLP(
    layer_dims=ARCHITECTURE,
    learning_rate=0.01,
    n_epochs=100,
    batch_size=512
)
mlp.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

mlp_train_time = time.time() - start_mlp
print(f"\nTraining complete in {mlp_train_time:.2f}s")

4.5 Evaluation & Comparison¶

In [26]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 4.5 Evaluation & Comparison
#
# Metrics computed from scratch (no sklearn):
#   MSE  = (1/n) * sum((y_hat - y)²)
#   RMSE = sqrt(MSE)
#   MAE  = (1/n) * sum(|y_hat - y|)
#   R²   = 1 - (SS_res / SS_tot)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def compute_metrics(y_true, y_pred):
    """
    Compute MSE, RMSE, MAE, R² from scratch.
    Parameters
    ----------
    y_true : array of actual values
    y_pred : array of predicted values
    Returns dict of metrics.
    """
    y_true = y_true.flatten()
    y_pred = y_pred.flatten()

    mse    = np.mean((y_pred - y_true) ** 2)
    rmse   = np.sqrt(mse)
    mae    = np.mean(np.abs(y_pred - y_true))
    ss_res = np.sum((y_true - y_pred) ** 2)
    ss_tot = np.sum((y_true - np.mean(y_true)) ** 2)
    r2     = 1 - (ss_res / ss_tot)

    return {'MSE': mse, 'RMSE': rmse, 'MAE': mae, 'R2': r2}


# ── Predictions ───────────────────────────────────────────
y_pred_lr_train  = lin_reg.predict(X_train_scaled)
y_pred_lr_test   = lin_reg.predict(X_test_scaled)
y_pred_mlp_train = mlp.predict(X_train_scaled)
y_pred_mlp_test  = mlp.predict(X_test_scaled)

# ── Compute Metrics ───────────────────────────────────────
lr_train_metrics  = compute_metrics(y_train, y_pred_lr_train)
lr_test_metrics   = compute_metrics(y_test,  y_pred_lr_test)
mlp_train_metrics = compute_metrics(y_train, y_pred_mlp_train)
mlp_test_metrics  = compute_metrics(y_test,  y_pred_mlp_test)

# ── Print Results Table ───────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 62)
print(f"{'Metric':<8} {'LR Train':>10} {'LR Test':>10} "
      f"{'MLP Train':>10} {'MLP Test':>10}")
print("-" * 62)
for m in ['MSE', 'RMSE', 'MAE', 'R2']:
    print(f"{m:<8} {lr_train_metrics[m]:>10.4f} "
          f"{lr_test_metrics[m]:>10.4f} "
          f"{mlp_train_metrics[m]:>10.4f} "
          f"{mlp_test_metrics[m]:>10.4f}")
print("=" * 62)
print(f"\nPrimary metric (RMSE) — Test set:")
print(f"  Linear Regression : ${lr_test_metrics['RMSE']:.4f} avg error per ride")
print(f"  MLP               : ${mlp_test_metrics['RMSE']:.4f} avg error per ride")

==============================================================
Metric     LR Train    LR Test  MLP Train   MLP Test
--------------------------------------------------------------
MSE         42.2055    42.4922     3.2068     3.3080
RMSE         6.4966     6.5186     1.7908     1.8188
MAE          5.1727     5.1883     1.2090     1.2179
R2           0.5141     0.5134     0.9631     0.9621
==============================================================

Primary metric (RMSE) — Test set:
  Linear Regression : $6.5186 avg error per ride
  MLP               : $1.8188 avg error per ride

plt.plot(lin_reg.loss_history, label='Linear Regression',
         color='steelblue', linewidth=1.5)

         color='darkorange', linewidth=1.5)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Plot 2: Performance Comparison Bar Chart — Test Set
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

metrics_list = ['RMSE', 'MAE', 'R2']
lr_vals      = [lr_test_metrics[m]  for m in metrics_list]
mlp_vals     = [mlp_test_metrics[m] for m in metrics_list]

x     = np.arange(len(metrics_list))
width = 0.35

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
bars1 = ax.bar(x - width/2, lr_vals,  width,
               label='Linear Regression', color='steelblue')
bars2 = ax.bar(x + width/2, mlp_vals, width,
               label='MLP', color='darkorange')

ax.set_title('Model Comparison — Test Set Performance')
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(metrics_list)
ax.set_ylabel('Metric Value')
ax.legend()

for bar in bars1:
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
            bar.get_height() + 0.01,
            f'{bar.get_height():.3f}',
            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9)
for bar in bars2:
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
            bar.get_height() + 0.01,
            f'{bar.get_height():.3f}',
            ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [29]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Plot 3: Actual vs Predicted — domain specific plot
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
y_true_flat = y_test.flatten()

for ax, y_pred, title, color in zip(
    axes,
    [y_pred_lr_test.flatten(), y_pred_mlp_test.flatten()],
    ['Linear Regression', 'MLP [14→64→32→1]'],
    ['steelblue', 'darkorange']
):
    ax.scatter(y_true_flat, y_pred, alpha=0.1, s=3, color=color)
    lims = [min(y_true_flat.min(), y_pred.min()),
            max(y_true_flat.max(), y_pred.max())]
    ax.plot(lims, lims, 'k--', linewidth=1, label='Perfect fit')
    ax.set_title(f'{title}\nActual vs Predicted Price')
    ax.set_xlabel('Actual Price (USD)')
    ax.set_ylabel('Predicted Price (USD)')
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [30]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Plot 4: Training Time Comparison
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
models = ['Linear Regression', 'MLP']
times  = [baseline_train_time, mlp_train_time]
colors = ['steelblue', 'darkorange']

bars = ax.bar(models, times, color=colors, width=0.4)
for bar, t in zip(bars, times):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
            bar.get_height() + 0.3,
            f'{t:.2f}s', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=10)

ax.set_title('Training Time Comparison')
ax.set_ylabel('Time (seconds)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Analysis¶

The MLP significantly outperformed Linear Regression across all metrics on this ride price regression task. On the test set, MLP achieved an RMSE of $1.82 compared to $6.52 for Linear Regression, a 3.6x improvement, and explained 96.2% of price variance (R2=0.962) versus 51.3% for the baseline.

This gap is expected given how ride pricing works. Price is structurally determined by ride tier, distance, and surge multiplier in a multiplicative, non-linear way. A Black car costs roughly 4x an UberX for the same distance, not just linearly more. Linear Regression assumes all feature contributions are additive and linear, so it fundamentally cannot capture these tier-distance interactions. The actual vs predicted scatter plots confirm this. Linear Regression predictions cluster in a narrow band regardless of actual price, while MLP predictions track closely along the perfect fit line across the full price range ($2.50 to $97.50).

The trade-off is computational cost. Linear Regression trained in 6.64 seconds while MLP required 57.88 seconds, which is 8.7x slower, due to backpropagation through two hidden layers and cache storage for all intermediate activations. At production scale with millions of rides, this cost difference becomes significant. However for the accuracy gains achieved here, the MLP is clearly the better model for ride price prediction.

get_assignment_results()¶

In [31]:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# get_assignment_results()
# Returns a dictionary summarising all assignment results.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def get_assignment_results():
    """
    Returns a dictionary summarising all assignment results.
    """
    results = {
        # ── Dataset Info ──────────────────────────────────
        'dataset_name'   : 'Uber and Lyft Dataset Boston, MA',
        'n_samples'      : len(df),
        'n_features'     : len(FEATURES),
        'problem_type'   : 'Regression',
        'primary_metric' : 'RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)',

        # ── Baseline Model ────────────────────────────────
        'baseline_model': {
            'name'              : 'Linear Regression (from scratch)',
            'learning_rate'     : lin_reg.lr,
            'n_epochs'          : lin_reg.n_epochs,
            'batch_size'        : lin_reg.batch_size,
            'train_MSE'         : round(lr_train_metrics['MSE'],  4),
            'train_RMSE'        : round(lr_train_metrics['RMSE'], 4),
            'train_MAE'         : round(lr_train_metrics['MAE'],  4),
            'train_R2'          : round(lr_train_metrics['R2'],   4),
            'test_MSE'          : round(lr_test_metrics['MSE'],   4),
            'test_RMSE'         : round(lr_test_metrics['RMSE'],  4),
            'test_MAE'          : round(lr_test_metrics['MAE'],   4),
            'test_R2'           : round(lr_test_metrics['R2'],    4),
            'training_time_sec' : round(baseline_train_time,      4),
        },

        # ── MLP Model ─────────────────────────────────────
        'mlp_model': {
            'name'              : 'MLP (from scratch)',
            'architecture'      : mlp.layer_dims,
            'hidden_activation' : 'ReLU',
            'output_activation' : 'Linear',
            'learning_rate'     : mlp.lr,
            'n_epochs'          : mlp.n_epochs,
            'batch_size'        : mlp.batch_size,
            'train_MSE'         : round(mlp_train_metrics['MSE'],  4),
            'train_RMSE'        : round(mlp_train_metrics['RMSE'], 4),
            'train_MAE'         : round(mlp_train_metrics['MAE'],  4),
            'train_R2'          : round(mlp_train_metrics['R2'],   4),
            'test_MSE'          : round(mlp_test_metrics['MSE'],   4),
            'test_RMSE'         : round(mlp_test_metrics['RMSE'],  4),
            'test_MAE'          : round(mlp_test_metrics['MAE'],   4),
            'test_R2'           : round(mlp_test_metrics['R2'],    4),
            'training_time_sec' : round(mlp_train_time,            4),
        },
    }
    return results


# ── Call and display ──────────────────────────────────────
results = get_assignment_results()

print("=" * 55)
print(" ASSIGNMENT RESULTS SUMMARY")
print("=" * 55)
print(f"  Dataset       : {results['dataset_name']}")
print(f"  Samples       : {results['n_samples']:,}")
print(f"  Features      : {results['n_features']}")
print(f"  Problem type  : {results['problem_type']}")
print(f"  Primary metric: {results['primary_metric']}")
print()
print("  BASELINE (Linear Regression)")
print("  " + "-" * 40)
b = results['baseline_model']
print(f"    Learning rate : {b['learning_rate']}")
print(f"    Epochs        : {b['n_epochs']}")
print(f"    Batch size    : {b['batch_size']}")
print(f"    Train RMSE    : ${b['train_RMSE']}")
print(f"    Test  RMSE    : ${b['test_RMSE']}")
print(f"    Train R2      : {b['train_R2']}")
print(f"    Test  R2      : {b['test_R2']}")
print(f"    Training time : {b['training_time_sec']}s")
print()
print("  MLP")
print("  " + "-" * 40)
m = results['mlp_model']
print(f"    Architecture  : {m['architecture']}")
print(f"    Hidden act    : {m['hidden_activation']}")
print(f"    Output act    : {m['output_activation']}")
print(f"    Learning rate : {m['learning_rate']}")
print(f"    Epochs        : {m['n_epochs']}")
print(f"    Batch size    : {m['batch_size']}")
print(f"    Train RMSE    : ${m['train_RMSE']}")
print(f"    Test  RMSE    : ${m['test_RMSE']}")
print(f"    Train R2      : {m['train_R2']}")
print(f"    Test  R2      : {m['test_R2']}")
print(f"    Training time : {m['training_time_sec']}s")
print("=" * 55)

  Problem type  : Regression
